dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a suspected stroke at --Address Redacted--.
Hello sir, my name is Malik. I'm an EMT with VCU EMS. You're safe now.
Left side numb, face drooping, speech slurred. Started an hour ago while I was resting.
Mr. Smith, what is your name and today's date?
John Smith, November 14th, 2019.
Can you move your right hand toward me? Then your left hand if possible.
Right hand fine... left won't move.
Eye opening is to verbal. Verbal response is oriented with full awareness of name and date.
Airway is patent and unobstructed at this time. No active stridor or signs of obstruction.
Breathing is even and at about 20 breaths per minute. No signs of labored breathing or cyanosis.
No wheezing or unusual sounds noted. Oxygen saturation remains stable at 99%.
Pulse on the right is 95 and regular. Left pulse is weaker but detectable.
You described sudden onset numbness, facial droop, and speech difficulty. Have these symptoms improved at all?
Slightly better, but still can't move my left arm well. Left side feels weak.
Are you allergic to any medications or substances?
Allergic to aspirin.
What medications are you currently taking?
I take Keppra regularly for seizures.
I see on your chart a past medical history of stroke from years ago. Is the current left-sided weakness different?
This is new. Different from what I used to have. Mostly in the left side.
When did the symptoms first appear and what were you doing?
Came on while I was watching TV at home. Very fast.
Since it began, how long did it last and has it completely resolved yet?
Still here now. Persisting.
Mr. Smith, have you noticed any neck pain or stiffness recently?
No neck issues at all.
Pupils are equal and reactive. No visual changes noted.
No drainage or unusual findings in ears or nose. Speech remained slurred, but intact.
Chest inspection shows normal expansion. No retractions or use of accessory muscles.
Breathing sound quality is unremarkable. Equal breath sounds heard bilaterally.
Left arm and trunk are noted to be weak, without angulation, swelling, or open wounds.
Current pulse rate is 95, regular rhythm, consistent with baseline.
Respiratory rate remains at 20. No signs of increased work of breathing.
Blood pressure is 151/78 mmHg. Within normal limits for you?
Blood sugar reads 98 mg/dL. No hypoglycemia detected.
Oxygen saturation is 99% while at rest. No additional oxygen needed at this time.
Cardiac monitor showing normal sinus rhythm with a increased heart rate at 95.
Preparing to place you on continuous EKG monitoring per stroke protocol.
Performing 4-lead monitoring. Remaining alerts to any cholinergic changes.
Beginning 12-lead EKG if patient can tolerate. Will obtain once fully immobilized.
The 12-lead has now been completed with your cooperation. Interpretation requested for accuracy.
12-lead EKG interpretation: no acute MI observed. Borderline signs for left atrial enlargement.
Preparing two attempts for IV access in your left arm due to concern for potential intravenous medications.
IV placed in your left wrist after one failed attempt on arm. No infiltration, secure and flowing normally. 18G needle established.
Mr. Smith, I need to move you to the stretcher now. That is okay with you?
Stretcher is ready to assist. We will move you securely.
Transport team will now place you on the stretcher and talk through each step.
Mr. Smith, feeling is from left side diminished, but no visible trauma observed during transfer.
All secured now with torso, leg, and head restraints in place.
All straps are secure, and you are now safely on the stretcher with continuous monitoring engaged.
Vital signs being tracked at all points during movement.
VCU Hospital has been alerted. Stroke alert activated at point of dispatch.
EKG and IV systems are both fully functioning. Rechecking status in 5 minutes.
Cardiovascular assessment now showing: pulse 70, BP 143/89, oxygen 99%. Stable and unchanged.
GCS reconfirmed at 15 (Eye 4, Verbal 5, Motor 6). Alert, awake, and response consistent.
Pulse remains steady at 70. Found from wrist and chest monitoring devices.
Respirations unchanged, exclusively at 20 per minute.
Blood pressure rechecked at 143/89. Within normal range for the patient.
EKG data reviewed again - no dramatic new findings reported yet.
Any discomfort reported? Mr. Smith, do you feel any pain or pressure right now?
Patient reports pain as zero. No new pain symptoms identified.
No development of new neurological complaints, motor improvement noted with transfer.
Current medications: Keppra for seizure management. Continuing use and no changes reported.
Improved speech clarity and subtle return in facial symmetry observed post-movement from floor.
Field impression is likely transient ischemic attack. No stroke signs on repeat neurological exam.
Patient is cooperating with treatment, denying new neurological deficits post-assessment break.
VCU is on notice and has stroke alert ready. 12-lead, IV, and EKG systems active at transport start.
Actual transport initiated with full protocol mobility systems engaged and active.
All critical tracking systems remain active. No transport interruptions.
Confirming pulse of 70, rate 20, and O2 saturation of 99. Vitals stable and consistent with transport requirements.
Past stroke effects noted on transfer, but not declining now from underlying baseline.
Previous CVA symptoms observed on exam, but further significant deficits have not been noted after reassessment.
Current IV in left wrist is ensuring effective fluid and potential medication routes for VCU.
EKG findings reviewed again for signs of infarction or further markers. No changes reported.
Maintain stroke protocol requirements and readiness for VCU upon arrival.
Transporting now with all systems confirmed operational and stable per EMS standard.
Patient remains on 12-lead monitor showing occasional supraventricular premature complexes and left atrial enlargement signs.
VCU is on alert for immediate evaluation upon arrival.
No additional neurological deficits have been identified during the transport duration and follow-up evaluations.
Following EMS stroke/TIA emergency pathway with transport under public health emergency measures.
